patterns.conditions: Useful in moments of profound ambiguity, paradigm breakdown, strategic surprise, failed diagnosis, and deep reflective practice where familiar concepts are no longer carrying the situation.
patterns.failure_modes: Romanticizing confusion for its own sake. Remaining in paralysis instead of letting the rupture produce new articulation. Treating every difficult case as a koan rather than sometimes just lacking information.
patterns.situation_signature: Every available framework fails simultaneously. The actor feels genuine confusion and pressure to seize the first available interpretation just to restore orientation. Yet the most important thing may be not escaping too quickly.
patterns.hot_signals: Repeated collapse of explanation. Strong discomfort at not knowing what kind of thing the situation is. Rapid oscillation among incompatible frames. A sense that old categories are no longer alive enough to carry the case.
patterns.leverage_points: Delay premature closure. Create protected time for disciplined non-resolution. Ask what the breakdown itself is revealing about the limits of existing categories. Let new articulation emerge after the rupture rather than against it.
patterns.koan: What becomes visible only after every available answer has failed?
patterns.canonical_example: A strategy team confronting a discontinuity in its market finds that none of its inherited models explain competitor behavior. The breakthrough comes only after the team stops forcing the anomaly into legacy categories and tolerates a period of genuine conceptual unknowing.
patterns.counter_example: A familiar problem that simply requires gathering missing facts or performing routine analysis. Not every uncertainty is a koan moment.
patterns.application_questions: Are we facing a true breakdown of frameworks or just incomplete information? What interpretation are we grabbing to escape confusion? What might become visible if we sustained uncertainty a little longer? How can confusion be held without collapsing into paralysis?
patterns.common_mistakes: Confusing epistemic rupture with vagueness. Worshipping confusion. Failing to move from breakdown to renewed perception and action.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Some of the most important perception changes happen only after the mind can no longer keep the world inside its old boxes. The discipline is enduring that interval without bolting back into counterfeit certainty.

patterns.name: Withdrawal
patterns.subtitle: Every object and situation exceeds any relation or analysis that encounters it
patterns.core_claim: No actor, object, institution, or situation is ever fully present to another. Every encounter reveals only a partial profile while something of the thing withdraws beyond access, use, and description.
patterns.structure: An entity enters relation: with an observer, a model, an institution, a market, a negotiation, or another object. The relation discloses aspects of the entity, but never the entity in its entirety. What appears in the relation is real yet partial. The withdrawn remainder is not a temporary knowledge gap waiting to be closed but a structural excess that exceeds any one frame of access. Analysis therefore always encounters manifestations, never total presence.
patterns.conditions: Useful wherever actors are overconfident that they have fully captured a person, organization, market, or situation. Especially relevant in strategic analysis, institutional diagnosis, and complex systems where models invite false completeness.
patterns.failure_modes: Turning withdrawal into epistemic fatalism. Using it as an excuse not to investigate. Romanticizing mystery instead of treating incompleteness as a disciplined constraint. Confusing structural excess with mere lack of data.
patterns.situation_signature: A situation where every explanatory model seems to work partially but never exhausts what is happening. Attempts at total capture repeatedly fail because the object of analysis continues to surprise, exceed, or resist the frame.
patterns.hot_signals: Persistent remainder after good analysis. Actors who continue to surprise despite extensive familiarity. Institutions that behave differently than their official self-description predicts. A feeling that what is most consequential is not fully available in current representation.
patterns.leverage_points: Hold models as partial disclosures rather than total captures. Use multiple frames without expecting final synthesis. Build strategies robust to residual unknowns. Respect the difference between what the situation is showing and what it is in excess of showing.
patterns.koan: What here remains real precisely because it is not fully available to your grasp?
patterns.canonical_example: A city government thinks it understands a neighborhood through maps, demographics, and formal stakeholder input, yet every intervention produces unforeseen reactions because the neighborhood as lived social object withdraws from the administrative frames used to know it.
patterns.counter_example: A constrained mechanical process whose relevant variables are fully specified for the decision at hand. Not every practical problem requires a metaphysics of withdrawal.
patterns.application_questions: What am I treating as fully knowable here? What partial profile am I mistaking for the whole thing? How can strategy remain intelligent under structural incompleteness? What surprises would reveal the withdrawn dimensions of this situation?
patterns.common_mistakes: Using withdrawal to make every problem mystical. Forgetting that partial access is still meaningful access. Treating the unknowable remainder as more important than the disclosed features you can actually work with.
patterns.practitioner_notes: The point is humility, not paralysis. A good model remains useful while admitting it never reaches the bottom of the thing. Often the most dangerous analysis is not the wrong one but the one that thinks it is complete.

patterns.name: Hyperobject
patterns.subtitle: Massively distributed forces are encountered only through local manifestations
patterns.core_claim: Some of the most consequential entities in a situation are so distributed across time, space, and scale that they can never be encountered directly as a whole — only through partial local manifestations that mislead if mistaken for the total object.
patterns.structure: A hyperobject operates across scales too large, diffuse, sticky, or temporally extended to appear in full at any one site of experience. Actors encounter symptoms, local episodes, or fragments and often treat them as self-contained. But the fragment is only one facet of a larger distributed entity whose behavior cannot be understood at the scale of the local manifestation alone. Interventions aimed only at the local site therefore often generate surprising results.
patterns.conditions: Useful when apparently local problems are actually facets of massively distributed systems: climate, capital flows, urban land markets, political culture, supply chains, platform ecosystems, or long-wave demographic structures.
patterns.failure_modes: Using hyperobject language to inflate every large problem. Losing operational focus because the distributed whole feels too vast to act on. Treating local intervention as meaningless instead of as partial contact with a larger entity.
patterns.situation_signature: A local intervention makes perfect sense within the immediate frame yet produces unexpected consequences because the force being touched far exceeds that frame. The local case is only one visible cross-section of something much larger.
patterns.hot_signals: Persistent mismatch between local fixes and system outcomes. Forces invoked abstractly but encountered only through fragments. Repeated surprise when seemingly bounded interventions trigger distant or delayed effects. Analysts arguing from the part as if it were the whole.
patterns.leverage_points: Map the scales at which the force operates. Distinguish local manifestations from the distributed entity generating them. Design interventions with awareness of delayed, distant, or cross-scalar effects. Avoid treating one visible site as the entire object.
patterns.koan: What larger thing is this local problem only a surface of?
patterns.canonical_example: A city tries to control housing affordability through one zoning adjustment and is surprised when broader capital flows, regional demand, financing structures, and political culture overwhelm the local intervention. The housing market is being touched locally but operates as a distributed hyperobject.
patterns.counter_example: A contained process problem inside a single office with clear local causes and effects. Not every difficult issue is hyperobjective in scale.
patterns.application_questions: What scales is this force operating across? What local manifestation am I mistaking for the whole object? What delayed or distant effects might follow from touching one facet of this larger system? How can a local intervention be designed with hyperobject awareness?
patterns.common_mistakes: Becoming abstract and grandiose. Treating scale as an excuse not to act. Assuming the distributed character of a force means no tractable leverage exists.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Hyperobjects are strategically important because they punish frame error. The intervention may still be local, but the intelligence guiding it cannot be. You need a local hand and a distributed model.

patterns.name: Constitutive Observation
patterns.subtitle: The framework used to see a situation also helps produce what becomes visible
patterns.core_claim: Observation is never neutral registration of a pre-given reality. The categories, apparatuses, and distinctions used to analyze a situation actively participate in producing what appears as the phenomenon under study.
patterns.structure: A situation is approached with concepts, instruments, classifications, and procedural cuts that distinguish signal from noise, actor from context, relevant from irrelevant. These cuts do not merely reveal what was already there in fully formed fashion; they help enact the phenomenon as an analyzable object. Different frameworks therefore do not simply offer competing descriptions of the same finished thing. They partially produce different realities of relevance, visibility, and consequence.
patterns.conditions: Useful in research, policy analysis, organizational diagnosis, performance metrics, regulation, and any setting where categories and measurement tools shape behavior and reality rather than just recording it.
patterns.failure_modes: Collapsing into total constructivism as if nothing constrains observation. Forgetting that constitutive observation still engages a real world. Using the concept to evade responsibility for making analytical distinctions.
patterns.situation_signature: Changing the framework changes not only interpretation but the phenomenon that becomes visible. Actors begin behaving to the metric, categories reshape incentives, and what counts as the problem shifts with the apparatus used to perceive it.
patterns.hot_signals: Metrics changing behavior. Formal categories making some harms visible and others invisible. Analytical disputes that are really disputes over what phenomenon is being enacted. Systems responding to how they are measured.
patterns.leverage_points: Intervene at the level of categories and measurement apparatus. Ask what your framework is making visible and invisible. Compare rival cuts to see what each enacts. Treat model choice as an intervention, not a neutral preliminary.
patterns.koan: What is your way of seeing helping to bring into being here?
patterns.canonical_example: A city evaluates neighborhood success primarily through tax base and development velocity, thereby producing a field where displacement, informal use, and social cohesion are backgrounded. The metric regime does not just describe success; it helps constitute what success becomes.
patterns.counter_example: A purely fictional invention unconstrained by any worldly resistance. Constitutive observation is not arbitrary invention; the world still answers back.
patterns.application_questions: What distinctions is my framework drawing? What does it make visible, and what does it disappear? How are actors adapting to the categories applied to them? What alternative cuts would produce a different actionable reality?
patterns.common_mistakes: Treating all observation as equally world-making. Failing to distinguish between more and less consequential apparatus effects. Using the concept to avoid making necessary analytical commitments.
patterns.practitioner_notes: This pattern turns reflexivity from virtue into necessity. If your framework helps constitute the field, then choosing a framework is already an intervention in the situation.

patterns.name: Nash Equilibrium
patterns.subtitle: A stable arrangement where no actor can improve by changing alone
patterns.core_claim: Systems can settle into stable strategic configurations that nobody likes much but nobody can improve unilaterally. Stability and optimality are independent; many equilibria are self-enforcing precisely because mutual best responses trap everyone in collective underperformance.
patterns.structure: Multiple actors choose strategies under mutual interdependence. Each actor's best move depends on what the others are doing. A Nash equilibrium is reached when no actor can improve their outcome by changing strategy alone while everyone else holds steady. The state can be efficient or disastrous; what matters is that unilateral deviation is punished. This makes equilibrium sticky even when all parties would prefer some coordinated alternative.
patterns.conditions: Useful in negotiations, market competition, bureaucratic stalemate, political polarization, arms races, and any strategic setting where actors are mutually responsive and individually constrained.
patterns.failure_modes: Assuming all stable outcomes are equilibria. Treating equilibrium as normatively desirable. Ignoring information problems, coordination barriers, and changing preferences that may destabilize the configuration.
patterns.situation_signature: Everyone complains about the arrangement, yet no one can move first without getting hurt. The outcome persists because each actor's individually rational adjustment still leaves the system stuck.
patterns.hot_signals: Collectively inferior stability. Repeated failure of unilateral reform. Mutual best-response logic. A system that feels locked even though alternatives are visible in principle.
patterns.leverage_points: Change incentives for multiple actors at once. Create coordination mechanisms for simultaneous shift. Alter payoffs so unilateral deviation becomes safer or more attractive. Stop trying to fix a strategic trap with moral exhortation alone.
patterns.koan: What makes this bad arrangement stable even though nobody actually wants it?
patterns.canonical_example: Two rival developers keep escalating amenities and concessions to win projects, shrinking margins for both. Neither can stop alone without losing bids, so the market stabilizes at a collectively worse equilibrium.
patterns.counter_example: A simple command hierarchy where one actor can unilaterally reset the arrangement with little resistance. That is not an equilibrium trap in the game-theoretic sense.
patterns.application_questions: What are the actors' best responses to each other right now? Why is unilateral improvement impossible? What coordinated move would escape this equilibrium? What payoff change would destabilize the current attractor?
patterns.common_mistakes: Calling every deadlock a Nash equilibrium. Forgetting that actors may misperceive payoffs. Treating stable outcomes as inevitable rather than engineerable.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Once you see an equilibrium, you stop telling actors to just behave better. The question becomes how to change the structure that makes the bad behavior individually rational.

patterns.name: Mechanism Design
patterns.subtitle: The deepest intervention changes the rules of interaction before play begins
patterns.core_claim: The most powerful strategic move is often not choosing well within a game but designing the game so that ordinary self-interested play generates better outcomes.
patterns.structure: Instead of treating rules, incentives, information flows, and decision procedures as fixed background, mechanism design asks how they can be engineered so that desired outcomes emerge from strategic interaction. Actors continue to pursue their own interests, but the structure channels those pursuits differently. The intervention therefore occurs upstream of behavior at the level of the architecture generating behavior.
patterns.conditions: Useful in governance, procurement, auctions, regulation, organizational design, platform rules, public process, and any setting where repeated strategic interaction is shaped by configurable rules.
patterns.failure_modes: Assuming design can eliminate politics. Overestimating the designer's knowledge. Building mechanisms too clever for the real informational and administrative capacities of the field. Ignoring that actors may game the mechanism itself.
patterns.situation_signature: A situation where exhorting actors to behave differently has limited effect because the structure rewards the current behavior. Better outcomes would require redesigning incentives, timing, information, or process rather than pleading for virtue.
patterns.hot_signals: Repeated strategic failure under stable rules. Good people producing bad outcomes because the game rewards it. Reforms targeting moves rather than structure. Actors behaving predictably rational within badly designed institutions.
patterns.leverage_points: Change the rules of choice, not only the choices. Reallocate information. Alter sequencing, thresholds, or reward structures. Engineer environments where better behavior is the strategically easier path.
patterns.koan: What if the real intervention is not one more move, but a different game?
patterns.canonical_example: A city redesigns a developer selection process so that long-term affordability and local hiring are embedded in scoring rules and disclosure requirements, instead of hoping bidders will voluntarily optimize for them.
patterns.counter_example: A one-off personal conflict where there is no durable rule structure to redesign. There mechanism design may be overkill.
patterns.application_questions: What game are actors actually playing? Which rules, incentives, and information structures generate the current outcome? How could the mechanism be redesigned so that ordinary strategic behavior produces a better result? What forms of gaming would the new mechanism invite?
patterns.common_mistakes: Confusing mechanism design with policy wish-listing. Believing a perfect mechanism can erase conflicting interests. Designing for ideal actors instead of real strategic ones.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Many failed reforms are just better arguments inside a bad mechanism. If the structure keeps reproducing the problem, stop optimizing play and redesign the field.

patterns.name: Signaling Theory
patterns.subtitle: Costly signals are credible because they are hard to fake
patterns.core_claim: When information is asymmetric, the credibility of a signal depends on what it would cost different types of actors to send it. Cheap signals are easy to fake; costly signals carry information because their cost sorts senders.
patterns.structure: One actor knows something another does not: quality, resolve, solvency, competence, intention. To communicate it credibly, the informed actor sends a signal. The signal matters only if sending it is differentially costly, risky, or difficult depending on the sender's true type. The more fakeable the signal, the less information it carries. Credibility therefore lies not in assertion but in the structure of costs around the assertion.
patterns.conditions: Useful in hiring, negotiation, politics, investor relations, coalition formation, and any setting shaped by asymmetric information and strategic self-presentation.
patterns.failure_modes: Treating all costly behavior as meaningful signal. Ignoring performative or subsidized signaling. Failing to ask for whom the signal is costly and under what conditions.
patterns.situation_signature: Actors make claims about quality, commitment, or intent, but the real diagnostic question is what it would cost them to make the claim if it were false. The signal is credible only if falsity is expensive.
patterns.hot_signals: Public commitments that would be embarrassing or costly to reverse. Investments that are hard to recoup. Sacrifices that low-quality actors would struggle to imitate. Markets full of talk but starved for costly demonstration.
patterns.leverage_points: Look past words to differential cost. Design processes that require credible signaling. Ask what cheap talk is being mistaken for information. Distinguish between commitment theater and genuinely separating signals.
patterns.koan: What would this message cost if it weren't true?
patterns.canonical_example: A developer claiming long-term commitment to a municipality offers unusually binding local hiring covenants with enforceable penalties. The value of the signal lies in the cost of noncompliance if the commitment is insincere.
patterns.counter_example: A press release full of optimistic language with no associated risk, cost, or binding consequence. That is cheap talk, not meaningful signaling.
patterns.application_questions: What private information is being signaled here? What would it cost this actor to send the signal if the underlying claim were false? Which current signals are cheap talk? How can the interaction be structured so credibility requires real sacrifice or exposure?
patterns.common_mistakes: Assuming sincerity equals credibility. Mistaking expensive posturing for informative signaling. Ignoring audiences for whom the signal is intended.
patterns.practitioner_notes: In strategic settings the question is rarely whether the statement sounds convincing. It is whether falsity is expensive. Once you learn to ask that, a great deal of rhetoric becomes informationally weightless.

patterns.name: Commitment Devices
patterns.subtitle: Voluntarily reducing future flexibility can increase present power
patterns.core_claim: An actor can make a promise or threat credible by deliberately removing their own future ability to back down. The loss of flexibility is precisely what produces strategic force.
patterns.structure: A statement about future behavior is not credible if the actor can later reverse it at low cost. A commitment device changes that by imposing a constraint: legal, reputational, institutional, procedural, or physical. Because retreat becomes materially harder, the actor's current declaration gains credibility. The paradox is that less future freedom can produce greater present bargaining power.
patterns.conditions: Useful in negotiation, deterrence, governance, personal discipline, institutional reform, and any strategic context where credibility depends on visible self-binding.
patterns.failure_modes: Locking into bad commitments. Mistaking performative rigidity for real constraint. Forgetting that some flexibility is strategically essential. Building commitments that work only symbolically and are easy to unwind.
patterns.situation_signature: An actor becomes more persuasive or intimidating not by appearing flexible but by making clear that retreat is genuinely costly or impossible. The self-binding itself is the move.
patterns.hot_signals: Binding public promises. Hard-to-reverse institutional steps. Legal or procedural locks. Actors voluntarily narrowing their own future options to alter current expectations.
patterns.leverage_points: Identify where credibility is weak because reversibility is cheap. Bind selectively. Distinguish cosmetic signals of firmness from actual self-limitation. Use commitment only when the value of credibility exceeds the cost of lost adaptability.
patterns.koan: What power would appear here if backing down stopped being easy?
patterns.canonical_example: A city publicly adopts a formal development framework with council approval and published milestones, making it harder for staff to quietly reverse standards under pressure. The public self-binding changes the bargaining landscape.
patterns.counter_example: An actor loudly declaring they will never concede while retaining easy private exit options. That is posture, not commitment.
patterns.application_questions: What future flexibility is undermining present credibility? What form of self-binding would actually constrain retreat? Is the gain in credibility worth the loss of adaptability? How could this commitment be gamed, reversed, or symbolically simulated?
patterns.common_mistakes: Celebrating commitment as such. Binding too early. Underestimating the value of reversible options in changing environments. Confusing reputational theater with real self-limitation.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Commitment devices are powerful because they transform words into structure. But they are dangerous for the same reason. Bind only where credibility matters more than adaptability and where you can live with the future you are making harder to escape.

patterns.name: Repeated Games
patterns.subtitle: The shadow of the future can sustain cooperation impossible in one-shot interaction
patterns.core_claim: When interaction is repeated and future encounters matter, cooperation can become rational because reputation, retaliation, and reciprocity alter present incentives.
patterns.structure: In a one-shot interaction, defection or opportunism may dominate. But when actors expect future encounters, today's move changes tomorrow's payoffs through reputation, trust, punishment, and reciprocal response. The longer, denser, and more certain the future relationship, the more room there is for cooperative strategies that would be irrational in isolated play. Repetition turns time into an enforcement mechanism.
patterns.conditions: Useful in long-term negotiation, procurement, vendor relationships, coalition politics, diplomacy, workplace culture, and any domain where actors repeatedly meet under memory and reputation.
patterns.failure_modes: Assuming repetition always produces cooperation. Ignoring asymmetry in patience, memory, or exit options. Forgetting that repeated games can also stabilize retaliation, cartel behavior, or enduring mistrust.
patterns.situation_signature: Present behavior makes sense only in light of expected future encounters. Actors accept short-term costs to preserve reputation, punish deviation, or sustain a relationship whose future value exceeds immediate gain.
patterns.hot_signals: Concern with precedent. Memory of prior rounds. Small present concessions made to preserve longer-term standing. Threats or promises whose force comes from future interaction rather than immediate payoff.
patterns.leverage_points: Increase visibility of future interaction. Make memory durable. Lengthen time horizon where possible. Use reputation deliberately. Recognize when one side can defect because they do not expect to return.
patterns.koan: How does tomorrow’s interaction change what is rational today?
patterns.canonical_example: A municipality and a major regional developer deal with each other across multiple projects over years. Concessions and hard lines in the current negotiation are shaped less by this project's immediate terms than by the reputational game governing all future projects.
patterns.counter_example: A truly one-off transaction between anonymous parties with no future contact and no reputational spillover. There the shadow of the future is absent.
patterns.application_questions: How likely is future interaction? How much does reputation matter across rounds? What punishment or reciprocity strategies become possible only because the game repeats? Which actors can escape the repeated game and therefore behave more opportunistically?
patterns.common_mistakes: Treating a repeated game as one-shot. Assuming all parties value the future equally. Forgetting that the same future shadow that stabilizes cooperation can also lock in vendettas.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Many formal agreements are weakly enforced by law but strongly enforced by the expectation of return. If the future matters, structure the present so memory and reciprocity do more of the work.

patterns.name: Schelling Point
patterns.subtitle: Actors coordinate without communication around what feels salient or natural
patterns.core_claim: In coordination problems without direct communication, actors converge on focal solutions that feel natural, salient, symbolic, or culturally obvious — not because they are formally superior but because they stand out as mutually expectable.
patterns.structure: Multiple equilibria are available. Communication is absent or constrained. Actors therefore search for features that make one option more noticeable, prestigious, legitimate, simple, or symbolically loaded than the others. A focal point emerges because each expects the others to recognize its salience too. Institutions, norms, and authorities can amplify certain focal points and function as correlation devices.
patterns.conditions: Useful in diplomacy, urban planning, crisis response, organizational coordination, public symbolism, market convention, and any setting with multiple possible convergence points and weak communication.
patterns.failure_modes: Treating focality as universal when it is culturally local. Assuming salience is natural rather than socially produced. Missing hidden authorities and precedents that manufacture what looks obvious.
patterns.situation_signature: Coordination happens around a solution no one formally negotiated because it somehow felt like the place to land. The important question becomes why that point was salient enough to organize convergence.
patterns.hot_signals: Reference to precedent, symbolism, or obviousness. Rapid convergence on one option among many equivalent ones. Institutions quietly shaping which outcomes feel natural. Shared cultural cues doing strategic work.
patterns.leverage_points: Make the desired outcome more salient. Use symbols, precedent, authority, and timing to create focality. Notice when the other side is winning by controlling what seems obvious rather than by formal argument.
patterns.koan: Why does this option feel natural enough that others will expect others to choose it too?
patterns.canonical_example: In a downtown redevelopment negotiation, a long-standing civic site becomes the default meeting point for compromise proposals not because it is objectively best but because everyone expects everyone else to treat it as symbolically central and politically legible.
patterns.counter_example: A pure distributive conflict where interests directly oppose each other and coordination on a focal point does not solve the underlying contest.
patterns.application_questions: What options are focal here and why? Which symbols, precedents, or authorities are manufacturing salience? How can a new Schelling point be created? Are actors mistaking cultural obviousness for objective superiority?
patterns.common_mistakes: Thinking focal points emerge from nature alone. Ignoring subcultures with different salience structures. Failing to see that institutions often work by making some equilibria feel like common sense.
patterns.practitioner_notes: A great deal of coordination power lies in managing what seems obvious. The actor who shapes focality often wins without needing to win the argument.

patterns.name: Principal-Agent Problem
patterns.subtitle: Delegated actors systematically drift when incentives and information diverge
patterns.core_claim: When one party acts on behalf of another under asymmetric information and imperfectly aligned incentives, distortions are structural rather than moral accidents. Trust helps, but cannot by itself eliminate the problem.
patterns.structure: A principal delegates authority to an agent. The agent has more local information, more direct control over action, and incentives that only partially overlap with the principal's goals. Because monitoring is costly and local knowledge is unevenly distributed, the agent can drift, shirk, overperform in the wrong dimension, self-protect, or optimize for visible metrics rather than the principal's deeper objective. The problem recurs because it is built into the structure of delegation.
patterns.conditions: Useful in management, governance, politics, legal representation, contracting, bureaucracy, consulting, and any situation where authority and action are separated.
patterns.failure_modes: Reducing all delegation issues to mistrust. Ignoring intrinsic motivation and professionalism. Treating agents as villains rather than as structurally positioned actors responding to incentives and information asymmetries.
patterns.situation_signature: An organization or principal keeps getting outcomes that no one explicitly wanted, because the delegated actor is optimizing for incentives, visibility, self-protection, or local rationality that diverges from the principal's actual purpose.
patterns.hot_signals: Metrics gaming. Information bottlenecks. Agents controlling what principals can see. Goodwill coexisting with systematic misalignment. Principals surprised by behavior that was predictable from the incentive structure.
patterns.leverage_points: Realign incentives. Improve observability without crushing local discretion. Change reporting architecture. Distinguish where trust is sufficient and where structural redesign is necessary.
patterns.koan: What is this agent rationally optimizing that the principal did not actually mean to ask for?
patterns.canonical_example: A public agency hires a consultant to represent community concerns in a redevelopment process, but the consultant's incentives are tied more to preserving the client relationship and producing smooth deliverables than to surfacing disruptive truths the principal actually needs to hear.
patterns.counter_example: A fully transparent setting where the actor and principal share incentives, information, and risk almost completely. There the delegation distortion is minimal.
patterns.application_questions: Who is principal and who is agent here? What information does the agent control? Where are incentives misaligned? What outcomes are being optimized because they are visible rather than because they are truly desired? How can the information architecture be changed?
patterns.common_mistakes: Asking agents to solve principal-agent problems with promises of goodwill alone. Over-monitoring until agent initiative disappears. Missing that principals also create the problem by choosing bad metrics and weak feedback loops.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Delegation always creates a second game. If you ignore it, you will keep diagnosing character where structure is doing most of the work.

patterns.name: Bargaining Theory
patterns.subtitle: Outside options and patience matter more than argument quality
patterns.core_claim: In negotiation, outcomes are shaped less by rhetorical brilliance than by threat points, outside options, time preferences, and the relative cost of no deal.
patterns.structure: Two or more actors have some potential zone of agreement. Each also has a fallback if agreement fails. The strength of that fallback and the actor's willingness or ability to wait shape bargaining power. An actor with better alternatives and more patience can extract more value because their cost of delay or impasse is lower. Formal arguments matter, but they are filtered through the deeper architecture of no-deal consequences.
patterns.conditions: Useful in commercial negotiation, labor relations, diplomacy, development agreements, coalition bargaining, and any setting where impasse is a live alternative.
patterns.failure_modes: Reducing negotiation entirely to BATNAs and timing. Ignoring legitimacy, symbolism, and relational dynamics that can modify perceived outside options. Mistaking bluffing about alternatives for actually having them.
patterns.situation_signature: A negotiation where one side seems rhetorically weaker but still extracts better terms because their outside option is stronger or their time horizon is longer. The visible conversation is less decisive than the invisible threat points.
patterns.hot_signals: Urgency asymmetry. One side repeatedly referencing alternatives. Visible impatience. A deal that looks substantively attractive but is weak because the no-deal option is tolerable for one side and intolerable for the other.
patterns.leverage_points: Improve your outside option. Increase patience. Reframe or strengthen the cost of no deal for the other side. Stop overinvesting in argument quality when structural leverage is the real determinant.
patterns.koan: What happens to each side if no deal is reached?
patterns.canonical_example: A municipality negotiating with a developer realizes that the developer's project financing clock is tighter than the city's political timeline. Once the patience asymmetry becomes clear, the city gains leverage without changing a single talking point.
patterns.counter_example: A purely expressive conversation with no option to agree or disagree on terms. There bargaining structure is absent.
patterns.application_questions: What is each party's outside option? Who is more impatient and why? What changes if the deal does not happen? Which claimed alternatives are real and which are bluff? How could the temporal structure be changed?
patterns.common_mistakes: Thinking persuasion can substitute for weak structure. Accepting the other side's urgency as your own. Failing to invest in outside options before entering the room.
patterns.practitioner_notes: The first mistake in bargaining is believing the negotiation starts at the table. Most leverage is built before the conversation begins, in alternatives, timing, and tolerance for no deal.

patterns.name: Coalition Formation Theory
patterns.subtitle: Coalitions persist only while members cannot do better by defecting
patterns.core_claim: Coalitions are stabilized not simply by shared purpose or size but by how value, risk, and indispensability are distributed among members. They fracture when subsets can improve their position by reconfiguring.
patterns.structure: Actors with overlapping but non-identical interests form a coalition to achieve outcomes unavailable alone. The coalition's persistence depends on whether each member receives enough value relative to their alternatives and whether key subgroups could improve their payoff by defecting or recombining. The coalition is therefore a dynamic bargaining structure, not a unified subject. Load-bearing members often matter more than numerically large but replaceable ones.
patterns.conditions: Useful in electoral politics, corporate alliances, social movements, executive teams, public-private partnerships, and multi-stakeholder governance.
patterns.failure_modes: Treating coalitions as morally unified. Ignoring side payments, recognition, and symbolic value in favor of crude seat counting. Mistaking numerical majority for structural indispensability.
patterns.situation_signature: A coalition looks stable in public but contains subsets whose departure would collapse the arrangement. Tension revolves around who is actually load-bearing, who is over- or undercompensated, and who could do better elsewhere.
patterns.hot_signals: Recurring demands from pivotal but underrewarded members. Public unity with private dissatisfaction. Fracture risk concentrated in strategically indispensable subgroups. Misreading size as leverage.
patterns.leverage_points: Map pivotality, not just membership. Track what each member loses outside the coalition and what the coalition loses without them. Redistribute value, recognition, or influence to load-bearing nodes before fracture occurs.
patterns.koan: Who matters here not because they are loud or numerous, but because the coalition changes shape if they leave?
patterns.canonical_example: A cross-sector redevelopment coalition appears dominated by major institutional players, but a smaller neighborhood bloc holds disproportionate leverage because its exit collapses the coalition's legitimacy with regulators and elected officials.
patterns.counter_example: A tightly hierarchical organization where subunits cannot meaningfully exit or recombine. Coalition logic may still appear, but formal coalition formation is not the primary dynamic.
patterns.application_questions: Which members are actually pivotal? What subset could improve its position by defecting? How is value distributed relative to contribution? Who appears central but is replaceable, and who appears marginal but is load-bearing?
patterns.common_mistakes: Counting heads instead of mapping indispensability. Assuming stated solidarity is enough. Waiting until open fracture to discover who the coalition actually depended on.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Most coalitions die from badly read contribution structures. If you do not know who the coalition loses most by losing, you do not know the coalition.

patterns.name: Zero Sum vs Positive Sum Identification
patterns.subtitle: Strategic intelligence begins by seeing where value is fixed and where it can be expanded
patterns.core_claim: The most basic strategic error is misidentifying the value structure of the situation. Treating a positive-sum space as zero-sum leaves value unrealized; treating a zero-sum contest as positive-sum invites exploitation.
patterns.structure: A situation contains some distribution of possible value. In zero-sum zones, one actor's gain is another's loss. In positive-sum zones, reframing, sequencing, exchange, or innovation can enlarge the total value available. Many real situations contain both structures at different layers simultaneously. Strategic intelligence lies in distinguishing them rather than applying one frame universally.
patterns.conditions: Useful in negotiation, market competition, coalition design, diplomacy, organizational resource allocation, and strategic planning.
patterns.failure_modes: Assuming everything can be reframed into win-win. Assuming hard conflict means every layer is zero-sum. Failing to separate symbolic, material, temporal, and procedural dimensions that may have different value structures.
patterns.situation_signature: Parties talk past each other because one is protecting a truly fixed stake while another is exploring expandable value elsewhere. The real task is diagnosing which parts of the situation are distributive and which are integrative.
patterns.hot_signals: False win-win rhetoric. Needless deadlock over issues that could be recombined. Naive cooperation in areas where predation is structurally rewarded. Mixed conflicts where some layers are expandable and others are not.
patterns.leverage_points: Disaggregate the problem. Identify fixed-value zones and expandable-value zones. Use reframing where possible and hard bargaining where necessary. Refuse one-dimensional narratives of the whole situation.
patterns.koan: Which part of this fight is truly over slices, and which part is about baking a larger pie?
patterns.canonical_example: A development negotiation contains zero-sum conflict over a limited land envelope but positive-sum opportunities around phasing, infrastructure coordination, community benefits, and long-term value creation. Failure comes when the whole negotiation is framed as only one type.
patterns.counter_example: A literal one-dollar auction for a single indivisible dollar. That is fully zero-sum for the relevant resource.
patterns.application_questions: What value here is fixed and what value could be expanded? Are different layers of the situation playing different sum structures? Where are we leaving value on the table by assuming pure conflict? Where are we being exploited by assuming expandability where none exists?
patterns.common_mistakes: Using positive-sum language as moral vanity. Refusing to acknowledge real distributive conflict. Treating mixed-sum situations as if one logic governs every layer.
patterns.practitioner_notes: This pattern is deceptively basic. Many sophisticated failures begin with misclassified game structure. Before you optimize a move, make sure you know what kind of game you are in and at what level.

patterns.name: Strategic Ambiguity
patterns.subtitle: Preserved uncertainty can be a source of leverage, deterrence, and optionality
patterns.core_claim: Clarification is not always strength. Under some conditions, deliberately preserving uncertainty about intentions, capabilities, or commitments creates leverage by preventing others from optimizing their response against you.
patterns.structure: An actor could specify their intentions, red lines, commitments, or capacities. Doing so would reduce uncertainty but also reduce optionality and enable strategic counter-adjustment by others. By maintaining calibrated ambiguity, the actor preserves maneuver room, deterrent haze, or bargaining leverage. The value of ambiguity lies not in confusion for its own sake but in making the other side calculate under uncertainty.
patterns.conditions: Useful in diplomacy, negotiation, litigation, platform strategy, organizational politics, and any setting where over-clarity can make you easier to exploit.
patterns.failure_modes: Using ambiguity where credibility is needed. Mistaking vagueness for strategy. Generating mistrust because ambiguity becomes indistinguishable from evasion or bad faith. Holding ambiguity too long and losing coordination with allies.
patterns.situation_signature: The actor gains by not fully clarifying what they will do, what they can accept, or where their limits lie. Clarification would collapse useful uncertainty and let others adapt too efficiently.
patterns.hot_signals: Pressure to state red lines. Opponents trying to pin down commitments. Ambiguity preserving coalition unity among internally divided actors. Situations where revelation would reduce leverage immediately.
patterns.leverage_points: Distinguish where ambiguity adds value from where it destroys trust. Keep uncertainty calibrated rather than total. Pair ambiguity with enough credibility that it is read as strategic rather than confused. Clarify only when commitment now produces more value than optionality.
patterns.koan: What power disappears the moment you explain yourself too clearly?
patterns.canonical_example: A public official in a multi-party redevelopment process refuses to fully specify which concessions would unlock approval, preserving room to maneuver among stakeholders and preventing the developer from optimizing a minimum-compliance strategy too early.
patterns.counter_example: A safety protocol where uncertainty about commitments would directly increase risk and coordination failure. There clarity is the source of power.
patterns.application_questions: What value is current ambiguity preserving? Who benefits if I clarify? At what point does ambiguity stop being leverage and become mistrust or drift? Is this a situation calling for optionality or credible commitment?
patterns.common_mistakes: Calling lack of thought 'strategic ambiguity.' Overusing uncertainty until partners stop trusting you. Missing the moment when leverage now requires commitment instead of concealment.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Ambiguity is like reserve power: valuable because it is not spent. But if used indiscriminately it corrodes coordination. The art is knowing when not being pinned down is stronger than being understood.

patterns.name: Prisoner's Dilemma
patterns.subtitle: Individually rational defection produces collectively inferior outcomes
patterns.core_claim: Actors do not need to be malicious to produce bad collective outcomes. When incentives reward unilateral defection, individually rational actors can systematically generate results that are worse for everyone than mutual cooperation would have been.
patterns.structure: Two actors each choose between cooperation and defection. Whatever the other does, defection yields the better individual payoff. This makes mutual defection the equilibrium even though both actors would be better off if both cooperated. The trap arises not from bad character but from the payoff structure, which punishes unilateral trust and rewards opportunism.
patterns.conditions: Useful in settings where cooperation is beneficial but fragile, where trust is difficult, and where one side can gain by defecting while the other cooperates. Common in competition, arms races, labor relations, and public-private bargaining.
patterns.failure_modes: Seeing every trust problem as a prisoner's dilemma. Ignoring repeated interaction, reputation, and institutions that change the game. Treating moral exhortation as enough to escape structural incentive traps.
patterns.situation_signature: Both sides would be better off cooperating, but neither can safely move first because unilateral cooperation is too exploitable. The resulting bad equilibrium persists because defection remains individually rational.
patterns.hot_signals: Low trust. Strong temptation to defect. Mutual complaints about a bad outcome nobody can escape alone. Cooperation attempts punished when not reciprocated.
patterns.leverage_points: Change the payoff structure. Increase transparency, monitoring, repetition, or enforceability. Reduce the reward to defection or the risk of unilateral cooperation. Stop moralizing what is structurally incentivized.
patterns.koan: What if everyone is behaving rationally inside a game that punishes the outcome they all prefer?
patterns.canonical_example: Two rival developers in a hot market each keep overbidding land and underpricing risk to stay competitive. Both would prefer a calmer market with better margins, but unilateral restraint would simply lose deals, so the collectively worse dynamic persists.
patterns.counter_example: A stag hunt where cooperation is preferred and defection is not tempting once assurance exists. That is an assurance problem, not a prisoner's dilemma.
patterns.application_questions: What are the payoffs to cooperation and defection? Why is unilateral cooperation unsafe? How could repetition, monitoring, or enforcement change the game? What behaviors are being condemned morally that are actually structurally rational?
patterns.common_mistakes: Calling any conflict a prisoner's dilemma. Ignoring the possibility of redesigning the game. Assuming trust alone can solve what incentives constantly undermine.
patterns.practitioner_notes: The prisoner's dilemma is clarifying because it moves diagnosis away from personality and toward structure. Once you see the incentive trap, the question becomes how to redesign the game so cooperation stops being a sucker's move.

patterns.name: Stag Hunt
patterns.subtitle: The superior outcome depends on mutual confidence, not incentive alignment alone
patterns.core_claim: Actors can achieve a high-value cooperative outcome only if each trusts the other to commit. The core problem is not temptation to defect for advantage but fear of being stranded if the other fails to show up.
patterns.structure: Two actors can coordinate on a high-payoff collective good, but only if both commit. If either defects, the cooperator does badly. Unlike the prisoner's dilemma, defection is not attractive because it dominates; it is attractive because it is safer under uncertainty. The central variable is assurance. Confidence above a threshold produces cooperation; below it, risk-dominant caution prevails.
patterns.conditions: Useful in coalition building, investment decisions, reform processes, collective action, and any setting where cooperation is clearly valuable but confidence in mutual follow-through is weak.
patterns.failure_modes: Treating a stag hunt as a prisoner's dilemma. Overengineering incentives when the real problem is assurance. Assuming verbal commitment is enough when actors still fear being the only one who shows up.
patterns.situation_signature: Everyone says the cooperative outcome is desirable, but no one wants to be first because the cost of unilateral commitment is too high. The barrier is confidence, not greed.
patterns.hot_signals: Interest in cooperation paired with hesitation. Repeated requests for proof that others will commit. Coordination failure despite aligned goals. Strong value placed on precedent and visible early movers.
patterns.leverage_points: Build assurance. Stage commitments visibly. Use pilots, guarantees, escrow-like structures, or early anchors that reduce fear of unilateral exposure. Focus less on incentives than on credible mutual confidence.
patterns.koan: What would make each side believe the other will really stay in for the larger prize?
patterns.canonical_example: Several institutions want to co-invest in a regional innovation hub, but no one wants to be the only early funder. Once a few highly credible actors make visible commitments, the larger cooperative outcome becomes reachable.
patterns.counter_example: A prisoner's dilemma where even with full assurance each actor still has incentive to defect. That is a different game.
patterns.application_questions: Is this a problem of temptation or assurance? What makes unilateral commitment feel too risky? What visible signals or staged commitments would raise confidence above the threshold? Which actors could serve as assurance anchors?
patterns.common_mistakes: Trying to solve assurance failure with motivational speeches. Confusing reluctance with selfishness. Underestimating how discontinuously cooperation can emerge once confidence crosses threshold.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Many failed coalitions are misdiagnosed because actors assume the issue is incentives when it is really assurance. If people already prefer the cooperative outcome, stop bribing them and start making follow-through credible.

patterns.name: Tragedy of the Commons
patterns.subtitle: Individually rational extraction destroys the shared resource everyone depends on
patterns.core_claim: When multiple actors can individually benefit from extracting more from a shared resource while the costs of depletion are socialized, the system tends toward collective ruin unless governance structures alter the incentives.
patterns.structure: A common resource is available to many users. Each additional unit extracted benefits the individual extractor more than it immediately harms them, because depletion costs are spread across the group. As each actor follows this rational logic, aggregate overuse degrades or destroys the resource base. The contradiction is structural: rational extraction at the unit level produces catastrophic outcomes at the system level.
patterns.conditions: Useful in natural resource management, shared budgets, public attention, institutional legitimacy, housing affordability, public space, and any domain where common resources are vulnerable to overuse.
patterns.failure_modes: Applying the commons frame to every shared problem. Ignoring successful commons governance. Treating the tragedy as inevitable rather than as a governance failure that can be addressed through rules, norms, and monitoring.
patterns.situation_signature: Everyone depends on a shared resource, yet each actor has reason to take a bit more than the system can sustain. Collective dependence coexists with individually rational depletion.
patterns.hot_signals: Diffuse benefit from restraint, concentrated benefit from extraction. Progressive degradation no single actor feels responsible for. Repeated calls for restraint that fail because defectors gain.
patterns.leverage_points: Create governance, monitoring, graduated sanctions, and local norm enforcement. Clarify boundaries and extraction rights. Increase visibility of cumulative damage. Shift the game from ungoverned extraction to collectively enforced stewardship.
patterns.koan: What shared thing is everyone rationally destroying because each actor can take just a little more?
patterns.canonical_example: A city's affordable housing stock erodes as each project individually rationalizes higher-end extraction of land value, while the long-term social cost of reduced affordability is distributed across the whole urban system.
patterns.counter_example: A public goods problem where the issue is under-contribution to a non-rival benefit rather than over-extraction from a rival shared resource.
patterns.application_questions: What is the common resource here? How are extraction benefits and depletion costs distributed? What local governance structures could change behavior? Is this actually a commons tragedy or a different collective action problem?
patterns.common_mistakes: Moralizing users instead of analyzing the governance failure. Confusing all shared resources with open-access anarchy. Ignoring the possibility of robust collective management.
patterns.practitioner_notes: The tragedy is not human selfishness in the abstract. It is a specific structure of incentives around a shared resource. Once you see that, the important question becomes what governance architecture can make stewardship rational.

patterns.name: Chicken
patterns.subtitle: Escalation wins only if the other side believes you truly cannot back down
patterns.core_claim: In brinkmanship conflicts, the advantage goes to the actor who most credibly convinces the other that retreat is impossible — but this same logic creates escalation traps where catastrophe becomes more likely as credibility increases.
patterns.structure: Two actors move toward collision. If one swerves and the other does not, the non-swerver gains prestige or leverage. If both swerve, neither gains decisively. If neither swerves, both suffer catastrophe. Success therefore depends on convincing the other side that you will not or cannot back down. Commitment devices become central, but the more credible they are, the harder it becomes to stop escalation once conditions change.
patterns.conditions: Useful in brinkmanship, labor standoffs, political crisis, deterrence, high-stakes negotiations, and reputational contests where yielding is costly but mutual escalation is catastrophic.
patterns.failure_modes: Treating every hard negotiation as chicken. Mistaking ordinary firmness for brinkmanship. Forgetting that some actors may value the crash differently, changing the game entirely.
patterns.situation_signature: An interaction where both sides are publicly escalating, retreat is humiliating, and leverage depends on demonstrating inability or unwillingness to compromise even as collision risk rises.
patterns.hot_signals: Publicly narrowed options. Escalatory rhetoric. Value placed on resolve displays. Commitment devices that remove face-saving exits. A mounting sense that nobody can back down without major loss.
patterns.leverage_points: Create off-ramps before commitments harden. Distinguish symbolic firmness from irreversible escalation. Read asymmetries in crash tolerance. Understand when showing flexibility is safer than winning the resolve contest.
patterns.koan: What kind of power is created here by making retreat impossible — and what catastrophe comes with it?
patterns.canonical_example: Two political actors escalate a zoning conflict into a public showdown where each signals that concession is impossible. The formal issue becomes less important than who is seen to blink first, even as mutual institutional damage rises.
patterns.counter_example: A war of attrition where the contest is about who can endure costs longest rather than about catastrophic collision from mutual non-retreat.
patterns.application_questions: Is this really a brinkmanship game? What makes swerving costly? Who can most credibly commit not to back down? What off-ramps still exist? How are the costs of collision distributed?
patterns.common_mistakes: Admiring resolve without pricing catastrophe. Escalating to prove credibility when quieter leverage would suffice. Failing to see when both actors are trapped by their own self-binding.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Chicken rewards credible madness or self-binding, which is why it is so dangerous. The strategic task is often to restore exits before the game locks into its most theatrical and destructive form.

patterns.name: Assurance Game
patterns.subtitle: Cooperation cascades once confidence crosses threshold
patterns.core_claim: Many collective shifts depend less on changing preferences than on raising confidence above a threshold at which cooperation, participation, or investment suddenly becomes self-reinforcing.
patterns.structure: Actors prefer to cooperate or participate if enough others do likewise, but below a confidence threshold they hold back. Because each actor's choice depends on expected participation by others, behavior can remain stuck in low-cooperation equilibrium until assurance rises just enough to tip the system. Above that threshold, cooperation or contribution cascades. The dynamics are discontinuous rather than linear.
patterns.conditions: Useful in mobilization, reform coalitions, investment waves, norm shifts, public participation, and any domain where collective takeoff depends on confidence that enough others will join.
patterns.failure_modes: Treating assurance problems as pure incentive problems. Ignoring threshold heterogeneity among actors. Assuming everyone needs the same level of confidence to move.
patterns.situation_signature: A system that stays inert until a few visible confidence shifts suddenly trigger much broader participation. The key variable is not desire but whether enough people believe enough others are in.
patterns.hot_signals: Latent willingness with low visible participation. Heavy sensitivity to public signals and early movers. Sudden nonlinear shifts after modest confidence gains. Repeated refrain that people would join 'if they knew others would too.'
patterns.leverage_points: Increase visible participation. Stage credible early commitments. Publicize threshold-crossing signals. Identify actors whose movement disproportionately raises collective confidence.
patterns.koan: What small increase in confidence would make cooperation suddenly become contagious?
patterns.canonical_example: A reform effort inside a bureaucracy seems stalled until a handful of respected senior figures openly endorse it, pushing the assurance level over threshold and making broader participation suddenly safe.
patterns.counter_example: A prisoner's dilemma where even high confidence in others' cooperation does not remove the temptation to defect. That is not primarily an assurance game.
patterns.application_questions: What is the relevant confidence threshold here? Who are the visible early movers that would change everyone's expectations? What signs would make participation feel safe? Where is latent support trapped below assurance threshold?
patterns.common_mistakes: Trying to pay for what should be unlocked through confidence. Ignoring visibility and symbolic endorsement. Expecting gradual uptake where threshold dynamics dominate.
patterns.practitioner_notes: When assurance is the bottleneck, the entire strategy changes. The task is no longer changing preferences but altering expectations about what others will do.

patterns.name: Public Goods Game
patterns.subtitle: Shared benefits are systematically underprovided when contribution is voluntary
patterns.core_claim: When benefits are shared regardless of who contributes, rational actors face incentives to free ride, producing chronic underprovision of goods everyone wants and depends on.
patterns.structure: A public good is non-rival and difficult to exclude others from once provided. Each actor benefits whether or not they contribute, which makes withholding contribution individually tempting. But if too many actors free ride, the good is undersupplied or collapses entirely. The problem is therefore not extraction from a shared stock but contribution to a shared benefit.
patterns.conditions: Useful in taxation, public infrastructure, civic capacity, collective knowledge, coalition maintenance, neighborhood improvements, and any setting where contributions generate broadly shared gains.
patterns.failure_modes: Confusing public goods with commons. Assuming free riders are morally deficient rather than structurally induced. Ignoring selective incentives, norms, and enforcement that can sustain contribution.
patterns.situation_signature: Everyone agrees the shared good matters, yet contributions are chronically too low because each actor prefers that others bear more of the cost.
patterns.hot_signals: Complaints about underfunded shared infrastructure. Reliance on a few overcontributors. Diffuse benefits with concentrated contribution costs. Normative appeals failing to solve contribution gaps.
patterns.leverage_points: Create selective incentives, obligations, prestige, sanctions, or governance rules that increase contribution. Reduce free-rider invisibility. Differentiate between willingness to enjoy the good and willingness to fund it.
patterns.koan: What shared thing does everyone want but no one wants to pay enough to sustain?
patterns.canonical_example: A regional innovation network depends on data sharing, convening, and ecosystem maintenance that benefit all participants, but contribution remains underprovided because each institution prefers others to carry the coordination burden.
patterns.counter_example: A tragedy of the commons where the issue is overuse of a rival resource, not under-contribution to a non-rival benefit.
patterns.application_questions: What is the public good here? Why is contribution structurally unattractive? Who is currently overcontributing? What governance or incentive architecture could make contribution more rational and visible?
patterns.common_mistakes: Moralizing free riding without changing the structure. Treating every shared-value problem as a public good. Ignoring how prestige and identity can sometimes substitute for formal enforcement.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Public goods problems often hide behind language of shared mission. Shared desire is not shared contribution. If the good matters, the question is how to make its support structurally reliable rather than morally aspirational.

patterns.name: Trust Game
patterns.subtitle: Cooperation begins when one actor accepts vulnerability before reciprocity is guaranteed
patterns.core_claim: Trust is structurally sequential: one party must move first and become vulnerable before the other can reciprocate. The possibility of exploitation is not noise but the very condition of trust's generative power.
patterns.structure: One actor decides whether to extend trust by transferring value, authority, vulnerability, or initiative to another. The second actor then decides whether to reciprocate or exploit. The first move creates the conditions of possible cooperation but also exposes the initiator to loss. Trust therefore depends not only on incentives but on how systems manage sequential vulnerability over time.
patterns.conditions: Useful in institutional trust, delegation, finance, relationships, coalition building, procurement, and any setting where one side must move first without full guarantee.
patterns.failure_modes: Treating trust as a vague sentiment instead of a structural sequence. Demanding trust without exposure. Ignoring how repeated betrayal or asymmetrical vulnerability alters the entire architecture.
patterns.situation_signature: A productive relationship cannot begin until someone accepts first-mover vulnerability. The system stalls because everyone wants trust from others without being the one who must risk first.
patterns.hot_signals: Calls for trust without meaningful exposure. Overemphasis on reassurance. First-mover hesitation. Repeated failure to create reciprocity because no one wants to bear initial vulnerability.
patterns.leverage_points: Reduce first-mover risk. Create reciprocal safeguards. Use repeated interactions, escrow, pilot commitments, and visible reciprocation to make sequential vulnerability survivable. Identify who is already carrying disproportionate exposure.
patterns.koan: Who must become vulnerable first for cooperation to become possible here?
patterns.canonical_example: A municipality and developer want a more collaborative process, but neither wants to reveal key information first. Trust begins only when one side makes a bounded, costly disclosure that invites reciprocity without fully surrendering protection.
patterns.counter_example: A simultaneous-choice situation where no party must move first or expose themselves before others act. That is not primarily a trust game.
patterns.application_questions: What exactly is the first-mover vulnerability? Why is no one willing to bear it? What structures could lower the cost of trusting first? Has previous exploitation made the current trust threshold much higher?
patterns.common_mistakes: Telling actors to trust more without changing the vulnerability architecture. Confusing politeness with trust. Ignoring how asymmetries in exposure shape willingness to move first.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Trust is costly because it is temporal. Someone has to go first. If your process requires trust, design for the actual problem of sequential exposure rather than invoking trust as a moral atmosphere.

patterns.name: Ultimatum Game
patterns.subtitle: Fairness norms can override narrow material rationality
patterns.core_claim: People do not negotiate only over material gain. They will often reject beneficial offers if those offers violate fairness norms, dignity, or identity strongly enough, which means social meaning is a structural force in bargaining.
patterns.structure: One actor proposes a division of a resource. The second actor can accept, in which case both get the proposed shares, or reject, in which case both get nothing. Classical narrow rationality predicts acceptance of any positive offer. Empirically, however, low offers are often rejected because unfairness itself is costly. This means bargaining is shaped not just by resource distribution but by norms of legitimacy, respect, and acceptable recognition.
patterns.conditions: Useful in labor negotiations, compensation design, public settlements, coalition bargaining, and any context where nominally efficient offers fail because they are perceived as insulting or illegitimate.
patterns.failure_modes: Treating fairness as irrational noise. Overgeneralizing laboratory results to every context. Ignoring that fairness norms vary by culture, identity, and power relation.
patterns.situation_signature: A materially positive offer is refused because acceptance would register as humiliation, disrespect, or illegitimate treatment. The conflict is not only over amount but over what the amount means.
patterns.hot_signals: Strong reaction to distributional symbolism. Offers rejected despite visible cost to the rejector. Repeated insistence that 'it's the principle.' Bargaining breakdown caused by perceived insult rather than absolute scarcity.
patterns.leverage_points: Price the symbolic meaning of the offer, not just its numeric value. Avoid proposals that humiliate. Use process fairness, explanation, and recognition to make terms acceptable. Ask what distribution communicates about status and legitimacy.
patterns.koan: What is this offer saying about them that makes it worth refusing even at a cost?
patterns.canonical_example: A public agency offers a neighborhood group a small benefit package in exchange for support on a major project. The group rejects it not because the money is worthless, but because the offer implies their standing can be cheaply bought and their concerns are being trivialized.
patterns.counter_example: A purely anonymous transaction with no fairness salience, no identity investment, and no future relationship. There narrow material rationality may dominate more strongly.
patterns.application_questions: What fairness norm is being violated? What symbolic meaning is attached to the current offer? Are we pricing only material value while ignoring dignity costs? How could the same material terms be structured to communicate legitimacy rather than insult?
patterns.common_mistakes: Thinking better math solves legitimacy wounds. Treating rejection as irrationality rather than as a norm-enforcing move. Ignoring the role of audience and identity in what counts as fair.
patterns.practitioner_notes: The ultimatum game is a reminder that actors defend meaning, not just money. In many real negotiations, the material offer fails because it says the wrong thing about status, standing, or recognition.

patterns.name: War of Attrition
patterns.subtitle: The outcome goes to the actor who can absorb the costs of waiting longest
patterns.core_claim: Some conflicts are not won by superior argument or immediate strength but by differential endurance. The side with greater cost tolerance, patience, or outside options can extract the outcome before the contest even appears resolved.
patterns.structure: Two actors pursue an outcome whose value may justify prolonged contest. Each round of delay imposes costs on both. The actor who exits first concedes, while the one who endures longer wins or extracts better terms. Because the crucial variables are relative cost tolerance, time horizon, and fallback options, the winner is often determined by endurance structure rather than by persuasive performance inside the contest.
patterns.conditions: Useful in lawsuits, labor disputes, procurement standoffs, political obstruction, market competition, and negotiations where delay itself is a weapon.
patterns.failure_modes: Treating every prolonged dispute as attrition. Ignoring symbolic or catastrophic brinkmanship dynamics that make it chicken instead. Forgetting that actors may misread each other's endurance and enter unwinnable contests.
patterns.situation_signature: The visible fight seems to be about principles or terms, but the deeper determinant is who can bear the costs of continued waiting. Endurance, not argument, is the central variable.
patterns.hot_signals: Long standoffs. High emphasis on patience and burn rate. One side relying on the other's urgency. Outcome determined by who can survive delay, not by who is more convincing.
patterns.leverage_points: Strengthen your outside option. Reduce your burn rate. Increase the other side's cost of delay. Avoid entering attritional contests you cannot outlast. Identify whether the real game is endurance before committing to it.
patterns.koan: Who can afford to wait longer, and what does that determine before the argument even starts?
patterns.canonical_example: A developer and city enter a prolonged negotiation over infrastructure obligations. The apparent dispute is over principles of fairness, but the actual determinant is whether financing deadlines or political timelines create greater cost pressure on one side.
patterns.counter_example: A chicken game where catastrophe follows mutual persistence rather than prolonged accumulating cost. Attrition and brinkmanship are not the same structure.
patterns.application_questions: What are each side's costs of delay? Who has the better outside option? What resources determine endurance? Are we in an attrition game or a different strategic form? How might cost asymmetry be changed before the contest deepens?
patterns.common_mistakes: Confusing stubbornness with strength. Entering attritional contests without measuring endurance. Focusing on rhetoric when burn rate and patience decide the outcome.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Many negotiations are quietly wars of attrition wearing the costume of principle. If you do not know who can endure, you do not yet know what kind of contest you are in.

patterns.name: Prospect Theory
patterns.subtitle: Losses and gains are evaluated relative to a reference point, not in absolute terms
patterns.core_claim: People do not evaluate outcomes in absolute terms. They evaluate them relative to a reference point, and losses weigh more heavily than equivalent gains, making framing and reference-point control strategically decisive.
patterns.structure: An actor faces a possible outcome. Instead of asking only what the outcome is, the actor implicitly compares it to a reference point: current possession, expected entitlement, prior status, imagined baseline, or promised future. Outcomes above that point feel like gains; outcomes below it feel like losses. Because losses loom larger than gains, the same objective state can produce different decisions depending on how the reference point is set and whether movement is framed as avoiding loss or achieving gain.
patterns.conditions: Useful in negotiation, policy design, compensation, organizational change, political messaging, and any setting where framing and perceived loss strongly influence behavior.
patterns.failure_modes: Treating prospect theory as if all decisions are just framing effects. Ignoring material constraints. Assuming loss aversion is fixed at the same magnitude in every context or culture.
patterns.situation_signature: Two parties react very differently to objectively similar outcomes because they are experiencing them relative to different baselines. A proposal fails not because it is bad in absolute terms but because it is coded as a loss.
patterns.hot_signals: Resistance to objectively beneficial change. Strong reactions to perceived loss of status, entitlement, or precedent. Framing shifts producing disproportionate behavioral shifts without changing underlying substance.
patterns.leverage_points: Reset the reference point. Frame proposals as loss avoidance when true. Surface hidden baselines and entitlements. Design transitions that reduce experienced loss rather than only increasing objective gain.
patterns.koan: Relative to what hidden baseline is this outcome being judged?
patterns.canonical_example: A city presents a revised development agreement as a package of new community benefits and gets weak uptake, then reframes the same terms as preventing the loss of public leverage and displacement protections that would otherwise disappear. The change in framing shifts the decision environment dramatically.
patterns.counter_example: A highly constrained technical optimization problem where the actor's reference point is irrelevant because only one feasible option exists. There prospect-theoretic framing has limited leverage.
patterns.application_questions: What reference point is each actor using? What is being experienced as loss rather than gain? How could the same substantive outcome be reframed without distortion? What transition design would reduce loss aversion enough to permit movement?
patterns.common_mistakes: Assuming better objective outcomes will sell themselves. Treating opponents as irrational when they are responding to loss aversion. Forgetting that your own framing is also reference-point dependent.
patterns.practitioner_notes: Many negotiations fail because one side is offering gains to people who feel they are being asked to absorb losses. If you do not know the reference point, you do not yet know what game of perception the actor is playing.

patterns.name: Dual Process Theory
patterns.subtitle: Fast intuitive cognition usually decides before slow reasoning arrives
